title: Boulevard/Intruder 800 carburetor issues — pilot jet and enrichment
description: The Intruder 800 (VS800, 1992-2004) and Boulevard S50 (2005-2009) use twin CV carburetors that clog from ethanol fuel during storage. The pilot jets are the first to clog, causing hard cold starting and rough idle. The enrichment (choke) cable stretches, reducing cold-start fuel delivery.
causes: Pilot jet clogging from ethanol fuel residue during storage
Enrichment cable stretched — insufficient cold enrichment
Float needle sticking from fuel varnish
Carb sync drifted between cylinders
fix_procedure: 1. Clean pilot jets — remove carbs, clear jets with wire and carb cleaner. 2. Check enrichment cable: ensure full travel at the carbs when lever is pulled. 3. Set pilot screw to 2.0-2.5 turns out. 4. Sync carbs with vacuum gauges. 5. For storage: petcock OFF, run until stall. 6. Forum tip: the Boulevard/Intruder 800 V-twin carbs are straightforward to service — only 2 carbs instead of an inline-4 bank. The pilot jets are the #1 clogging point. Pull the bowls, clean the jets, and the bike comes back to life. For storage, drain the carbs by running them dry — this single habit prevents 90% of seasonal starting problems. The enrichment cable stretch is sneaky — check that pulling the lever gives full plunger travel at both carbs.

title: Boulevard C50/M50 fuel injection lean surge
description: The Boulevard C50 (2005-2019) and M50 (2005-2019) use fuel injection with lean factory mapping. A rhythmic surge at steady cruise is the #1 complaint. The ECU's emissions-compliant map runs too lean at partial throttle, and the O2 sensor feedback creates hunting behavior.
causes: Factory lean FI mapping for emissions
O2 sensor closed-loop feedback hunting
TPS calibration slightly off
Intake air leak at throttle body boots
fix_procedure: 1. TPS adjustment: set to factory spec. 2. Dobeck EJK or Power Commander for cruise enrichment. 3. O2 sensor eliminator (resistor piggyback) for open-loop operation. 4. Check throttle body boots for cracks. 5. Forum tip: the Boulevard C50/M50 lean surge is the same issue as most Suzuki EFI bikes — lean factory mapping. The Dobeck EJK ($230) is the most popular fix. The O2 sensor eliminator ($20) forces open-loop operation, which also helps. The combination of EJK + O2 eliminator + PAIR block-off is the standard Boulevard EFI tune. Boulevard rider forums have model-year-specific EJK dial settings.

title: Boulevard C90/M109R shaft drive service
description: The Boulevard C90 (2005-2013) and M109R (2006+) use shaft final drive. The hypoid gear oil needs changing every 6,000 miles but many cruiser owners never change it. The M109R's 1783cc V-twin puts enormous torque through the shaft drive, accelerating gear oil degradation.
causes: Final drive gear oil degraded from neglect
M109R torque accelerating gear oil breakdown
Seal failure allowing water intrusion
Hypoid gears wearing from insufficient lubrication
fix_procedure: 1. Change final drive oil every 6K miles with 80W-90 hypoid gear oil. 2. Capacity is approximately 180-200ml. 3. Check drain plug for metal particles. 4. Inspect output seal for weeping. 5. Forum tip: the Boulevard shaft drive is nearly indestructible IF you change the oil. The M109R's massive torque demands the 6K mile interval — don't stretch it. A $5 oil change prevents a $1500+ final drive rebuild. Check for metal particles on the drain plug magnetic tip every change. Fine dust = normal; chips or chunks = damage requiring immediate attention. Put a sticker on the swingarm with the mileage at each change.

title: Boulevard M109R clutch drag and creep
description: The M109R's 1783cc V-twin produces massive torque that stresses the wet clutch. The clutch drags at idle, causing the bike to creep forward with the clutch pulled and the transmission in gear. This makes neutral difficult to find at stops and low-speed maneuvering challenging.
causes: Clutch basket notching from massive torque
Clutch cable adjustment insufficient for complete disengagement
Friction plate warping from heat
Steel plate scoring from debris
fix_procedure: 1. Adjust clutch cable: ensure full disengagement at the lever. 2. Check clutch basket for notching — file smooth or replace. 3. Replace all friction plates, steel plates, and springs as a set. 4. Consider Barnett or EBC heavy-duty clutch kit. 5. Forum tip: the M109R clutch drag is partly by design — the massive torque and heavy flywheel make complete disengagement difficult. Proper cable adjustment is the first step — ensure there's 2-3mm free play at the lever. If the clutch still drags with correct adjustment, the basket is notched or the plates are warped. The Barnett heavy-duty kit ($200-250) with heavier springs provides more clamping force and cleaner disengagement. Some M109R owners switch to a hydraulic clutch conversion for better modulation.

title: Intruder/Boulevard 1500 starter clutch failure
description: The Intruder 1500 (VL1500, 1998-2004) and Boulevard C90 use a one-way starter clutch (sprag) that wears. The starter motor spins but doesn't engage the engine, or engages intermittently. This is a labor-intensive repair requiring engine cover removal.
causes: Starter clutch sprag roller wear from age and use
Starter clutch spring fatigue
Oil contamination affecting sprag grip
High-compression engine stressing starter system
fix_procedure: 1. Symptom: starter spins freely without cranking engine. 2. Remove left engine cover and clutch assembly to access starter clutch. 3. Replace starter clutch assembly (sprag and rollers). 4. Inspect starter gear teeth for damage. 5. Forum tip: the Intruder/Boulevard 1500 starter clutch failure is the most expensive common repair on these bikes. The part is $150-200 OEM but the labor is 4-6 hours because the entire clutch assembly must come out to access the starter clutch behind it. If you're doing this repair yourself, replace the clutch components (plates, springs) at the same time since you have everything apart. Some owners learn to bump-start as a backup — the 1500cc engine starts easily in second gear at walking speed.

title: Boulevard/Intruder charging system — stator and reg/rec
description: The Boulevard and Intruder cruiser V-twins share charging system weaknesses with the rest of the Suzuki family. The stator windings overheat, the reg/rec fails, and the connector corrodes. Cruisers with highway bars, aux lights, and heated grips tax the charging system.
causes: Stator winding insulation breakdown
Reg/rec failure
Aftermarket accessories overloading charging
Stator connector corrosion
fix_procedure: 1. Test at 3000+ RPM: 13.5-14.5V at battery. 2. Test stator AC. 3. Replace both together. 4. MOSFET reg/rec. 5. Solder stator connector. 6. Forum tip: Boulevard/Intruder owners love accessories — chrome, lights, audio systems. The stock charging system doesn't love them back. Calculate your total accessory draw before adding more lights and speakers. A MOSFET reg/rec upgrade is especially important on cruisers with heavy electrical loads. The stator connector is the same weak point — solder it. For touring cruisers, a volt gauge on the handlebars ($15) lets you monitor charging in real time.

title: Boulevard/Intruder air-cooled V-twin heat management
description: Most Boulevard and Intruder models use air-cooled V-twins (C50, S50, S83) that rely on airflow for cooling. In traffic, the rear cylinder runs hotter than the front, and heat radiates onto the rider. Oil temperatures climb past safe limits during extended idle.
causes: Air-cooled V-twin depends on airflow for cooling
Rear cylinder gets less airflow than front
Traffic eliminates cooling airflow
Oil temperature exceeds safe limits at extended idle
fix_procedure: 1. Use full-synthetic 20W-50 oil rated for air-cooled engines. 2. In traffic: neutral at red lights, avoid extended idle. 3. Consider oil cooler for heavy commuter use. 4. Never idle more than 5 minutes — shut off the engine. 5. Forum tip: every air-cooled Boulevard cruiser runs hot in traffic — it's a characteristic of the design, not a defect. Full-synthetic 20W-50 is mandatory — it maintains protection at the temperatures these engines see. An oil cooler is the permanent fix for commuters ($150-200). The liquid-cooled M109R and C90 don't have this issue. For the air-cooled models, riding style matters: keep moving, shift to neutral at stops, and don't idle.

title: M109R fuel pump and FI relay
description: The M109R fuel pump is a high-flow unit feeding the massive 1783cc V-twin. The fuel pump relay and the pump itself are failure points. The M109R's fuel consumption is significant, and the pump must deliver high volume at full throttle.
causes: Fuel pump relay contact failure
Fuel pump motor wear from high output demand
Fuel filter clogging
Ethanol fuel degrading pump components
fix_procedure: 1. Key ON: listen for pump prime. 2. No prime: check relay first. 3. Test fuel pressure. 4. Replace pump assembly if needed. 5. Forum tip: the M109R fuel pump must deliver substantial fuel to feed 1783cc at full throttle. The relay is the first check as always ($15). The M109R-specific pump is more expensive than smaller Suzuki pumps ($200-300). An external inline fuel filter extends pump life. The M109R community has documented aftermarket pump alternatives from Walbro that flow even more fuel for modified engines.

title: Boulevard exhaust modification and fueling — popping and lean conditions
description: Boulevard cruiser owners commonly install aftermarket exhaust for a deeper sound. Without fueling adjustment, the bike runs lean, causing backfiring, decel popping, and potential engine damage. Carbureted models need rejetting; EFI models need a fuel controller.
causes: Aftermarket exhaust without fuel adjustment
PAIR system amplifying decel popping
Lean condition from increased exhaust flow
Air filter upgrade without matching fuel increase
fix_procedure: 1. Carb models: rejet — typically +1-2 sizes on main and pilot. 2. EFI models: Dobeck EJK or Power Commander. 3. Remove PAIR system: block-off plates. 4. Slip-on only: minimal change. Full system requires adjustment. 5. Forum tip: the correct order for Boulevard exhaust mods: (1) install exhaust, (2) remove PAIR, (3) adjust fueling. Doing only step 1 gives you a louder bike that runs worse. For EFI models, the Dobeck EJK is popular because it's a simple inline device — no laptop tuning needed. For carb models, the rejetting is well-documented on Boulevard forums with specific jet sizes for every major aftermarket exhaust brand.

title: Boulevard brake system — rear drum and front caliper
description: Some Boulevard models (S40, older S50) use a rear drum brake, while others use disc. The rear drum requires periodic adjustment and shoe replacement. The front disc caliper pistons corrode from neglected brake fluid. Cruiser riders often defer brake maintenance.
causes: Rear drum shoes worn below minimum thickness
Front caliper pistons corroded from old brake fluid
Brake fluid never flushed — water contamination
Drum brake adjustment neglected
fix_procedure: 1. Rear drum: adjust via star wheel or cam at the wheel hub. Check shoe thickness. 2. Front disc: flush fluid every 2 years with DOT 4. 3. Rebuild front caliper if pistons are sticking. 4. Replace rubber brake lines with stainless braided. 5. Forum tip: cruiser riders tend to use less front brake than sport riders, which paradoxically causes more corrosion — the caliper pistons sit in the same position and corrode where the seal sits. Regular use of the front brake actually exercises the pistons and keeps them cleaner. Flush fluid every 2 years regardless. For the rear drum models, adjustment is a 5-minute job that should be checked monthly. The drum shoes typically last 15-20K miles depending on riding style.
